questions.question: Explain the use of length() method in Java with respect to String class. Write a sample program to demonstrate its usage.
questions.hint: Remember that length() is a method (parentheses required) unlike array's length property
keyPoints: length() is a method of String class that returns total number of characters in a string
Return type is int
Syntax: public int length()
Example: String s = "Hello"; int len = s.length(); returns 5
Can be used in loops for iteration over string characters
Also works with StringBuilder to return number of characters

questions.question: Differentiate between length() method used for Strings and length property used for arrays in Java.
questions.hint: Think of String as an object with methods vs arrays as a special object with properties
keyPoints: String.length() is a method with parentheses
Array.length is a property (no parentheses)
Both return int value representing size/count
String length counts characters including spaces
Array length counts number of elements
String length cannot be modified directly; arrays have mutable length in certain contexts

questions.question: Write a Java program to check whether a given string is empty or not using the length() method.
questions.hint: Always check for null before calling methods on String objects
keyPoints: Use condition: str.length() == 0 to check if empty
Also check for null: str == null before calling length()
Alternative: str.isEmpty() method (Java 6+)
Handle NullPointerException possibility
Return boolean result indicating empty status

questions.question: Explain how length() method is used in string validation with an example of password checking.
questions.hint: Combine length() check with other String methods like charAt() for comprehensive validation
keyPoints: Password validation typically requires minimum length
Use length() to check if password meets minimum requirement
Example: if(password.length() < 8) then reject
Can combine with other checks (uppercase, digits, special chars)
Prevents weak passwords and improves security

questions.question: What is the output of length() method for the string "university 2022"? Justify your answer.
questions.hint: Count every character including spaces and digits
keyPoints: The string "university 2022" contains 8 characters
Characters: V, T, U, (space), 2, 0, 2, 2
Spaces are counted as characters
Digits are counted as individual characters
Returns int value 8

questions.question: Explain why length() returns 0 for an empty string and throws NullPointerException for null reference.
questions.hint: Understand the difference between null and empty string in Java
keyPoints: Empty string ("") is a valid object with zero characters
null string has no object reference, no memory allocation
Calling method on null reference causes NullPointerException
length() works on empty string object normally returning 0
Best practice: check for null before calling length()

questions.question: Explain the various String constructors in Java. Write a program to create strings using different constructors and demonstrate the use of length() method on each.
questions.hint: Review String class constructors from java.lang package
keyPoints: String() - creates empty string
String(String original) - creates copy of existing string
String(char[] array) - creates string from character array
String(byte[] bytes) - creates string from byte array
String(StringBuffer buffer) - creates from StringBuffer
StringBuilder builder) - creates from StringBuilder (Java 11+)
Program demonstrating length() on each constructor type
All return string with correct character count

questions.question: Design a Java program to count vowels, consonants, digits, and special characters in a given string using the length() method.
questions.hint: Use Character class methods for classification of characters
keyPoints: Use for loop with i < str.length() as condition
Extract each character using charAt(i)
Check if character is vowel (a,e,i,o,u) - convert to lowercase first
Check if character is digit using Character.isDigit()
Check if character is alphabet using Character.isLetter()
Otherwise count as special character
Display counts for all categories

questions.question: Write a detailed Java program to reverse a string without using built-in reverse() method, using length() in the iteration logic.
questions.hint: Start loop from length()-1 to access last character index
keyPoints: Initialize empty string for result
Use for loop: for(int i = str.length() - 1; i >= 0; i--)
Extract character from end using charAt(i)
Concatenate characters to build reversed string
Alternative: use StringBuilder and append in reverse order
Display original and reversed strings
Handle edge cases: empty string, single character

questions.question: Explain the relationship between String class and character arrays. How does length() help in converting between them?
questions.hint: String uses char[] internally, but exposes character operations through methods
keyPoints: String internally uses char[] to store characters
toCharArray() method converts String to char[]
String constructor accepts char[] as parameter
length() returns the size of internal character array
Use length() to determine array size when converting
Example: char[] arr = new char[str.length()];
Both maintain same sequence of characters

questions.question: Write a comprehensive Java program to validate user input for a username that must be 5-15 characters long and contain only alphabets using length() and charAt() methods.
questions.hint: Combine length() validation with character-by-character verification
keyPoints: Check length using: username.length() >= 5 && username.length() <= 15
Loop through each character using length()
Use charAt(i) to extract each character
Use Character.isLetter() to verify alphabetic only
Reject if conditions not met with appropriate error messages
Accept if all validations pass
Handle null/empty input gracefully

questions.question: Consider the following code snippet: String s1 = "Advanced Java"; String s2 = new String("Advanced Java"); System.out.println(s1.length()); System.out.println(s2.length()); What will be the output and why?
questions.hint: Count characters carefully including the space between words
keyPoints: Both strings contain same text: "Advanced Java"
s1 uses string literal - stored in string pool
s2 uses new keyword - creates new object in heap
Both have 13 characters (including space)
Output: 13 (for both println statements)
length() works identically regardless of string creation method

questions.question: Trace the execution of the following code and determine how many times the loop will iterate: String str = "university"; for(int i = 0; i < str.length(); i++) { System.out.print(str.charAt(i)); }
questions.hint: Loop runs from i=0 to i=length()-1, inclusive
keyPoints: String "university" has 3 characters
Loop starts at i = 0
Condition: i < 3 (i.e., 0 < 3, 1 < 3, 2 < 3, 3 < 3 false)
Loop iterates 3 times (i = 0, 1, 2)
Output: university (characters printed without spaces)
Each iteration prints one character using charAt(i)

questions.question: What will be the output of the following code? Explain the step-by-step execution: String s = ""; System.out.println("Length: " + s.length()); s = null; System.out.println("Length after null: " + (s != null ? s.length() : 0));
questions.hint: Remember null check is essential before calling methods
keyPoints: First string s = "" creates empty string object
s.length() on empty string returns 0
First output: "Length: 0"
Second assignment: s = null makes s reference null
Calling s.length() directly would throw NullPointerException
Ternary operator checks: s != null ? s.length() : 0
Since s is null, returns 0
Second output: "Length after null: 0"

questions.question: Given String str = "Programming", write a program to find the middle character(s) using length() method. What is the output for this string?
questions.hint: For odd length strings, there is one middle character; for even, there are two
keyPoints: String "Programming" has 11 characters
length() returns 11
For odd length: middle index = length/2 = 5 (0-based)
Character at index 5 is 'r' (P-r-o-g-r-a-m-m-i-n-g)
For even length: two middle characters at indices (length/2 - 1) and (length/2)
Use: int mid = str.length() / 2
Output: 'r' is the middle character
Program should handle both odd and even length strings
